food_subgroup: Handmade flat noodle soup; wheat-egg noodles in ikan bilis (dried anchovy) broth with minced pork, egg, sweet potato leaves, and crispy ikan bilis topping

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists specifically for Ban Mian. FOODON_03315393 (noodle dish) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code exists specifically for Ban Mian. A0DGM (Noodle dish) is the closest defensible parent.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Ban Mian or handmade noodle soup as a food concept.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard hawker bowl of Ban Mian weighs approximately 500g including broth, comprising roughly 150–180g of handmade noodles, 250–280g of ikan bilis broth, 50g of minced pork, one whole egg (~50g), a portion of sweet potato leaves (~40g), and a small serving of crispy ikan bilis (~10g).

nutrient_source.title: Ban Mian — HPB Singapore Foodo / Healthhub.sg
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Ban Mian (handmade flat noodle soup, 1 bowl ~500g including broth). Core macros and secondary nutrients are verified against this primary Singapore regional source. Micronutrients for copper, manganese, and vitamin K were supplemented from enriched JSON cross-reference with USDA FoodData Central component data for constituent ingredients (wheat noodles, pork, egg, leafy greens, anchovies) where HPB data was not granular; these are noted at confidence 0.9.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values — wheat noodle analogous estimate; Atkinson et al. (2008) and University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Singapore-style handmade Ban Mian. GI ~60 (medium) is estimated by analogy to wheat-egg flat noodles (GI 57–65 range) from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2008) international GI tables. The denser, hand-shaped structure of Ban Mian noodles (vs. machine-extruded noodles) is associated with marginally lower starch gelatinisation and a slightly lower GI than standard wheat noodles. The presence of protein (minced pork, egg) and fat (egg yolk, ikan bilis) in the bowl further attenuates the postprandial glycaemic response. GL per 500g bowl = GI × (carbohydrate_g_per_serving / 100) = 60 × (58 / 100) = 34.8.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content ~820mg per 500g bowl from ikan bilis broth and crispy ikan bilis topping
hypertension.guidance: Ban Mian delivers approximately 820mg sodium per bowl — lower than prawn noodle soup (1,200–1,800mg) but still meaningful for hypertensive patients; leaving half the broth reduces sodium intake by approximately 300–400mg, and requesting no crispy ikan bilis topping removes a further ~300mg sodium.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Glycemic load ~34.8 per bowl (GI ~60, ~58g carbohydrate per serving) primarily from handmade wheat-egg noodles
type2_diabetes.guidance: The medium GI (~60) and high GL (~34.8) of a standard bowl mean that portion control of the noodle component is the primary lever for glycaemic management; requesting a smaller noodle portion (half noodles) reduces GL proportionately, and the co-ingestion of protein from minced pork and egg and fat from egg yolk attenuates the postprandial glucose rise.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate sodium (~820mg/bowl), low saturated fat (~3g/bowl), and cholesterol (~250mg/bowl) from egg and minced pork
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Ban Mian has a relatively favourable cardiovascular profile among Singapore hawker noodle soups — saturated fat is low (~3g per bowl) and the ikan bilis broth provides calcium; however, the sodium load (~820mg/bowl) and dietary cholesterol (~250mg/bowl from egg and pork) should be considered for patients with established cardiovascular disease, and broth reduction and egg-yolk avoidance are practical modifications.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Moderate sodium, antioxidant micronutrients from sweet potato leaves, and calcium from ikan bilis broth
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Ban Mian is generally suitable for rheumatoid arthritis patients — sweet potato leaves contribute vitamin A (~120mcg RAE/serving) and vitamin C (~10mg/serving) with mild antioxidant benefit, the egg provides vitamin D (~1.5mcg/serving) relevant for RA-associated bone health, and the ikan bilis broth supplies calcium (~175mg/bowl); sodium (~820mg/bowl) is manageable for RA patients not on high-dose corticosteroids.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Ban Mian (板面 / 板麵, Bǎn Miàn) is a Singapore-Hakka hawker noodle soup made with hand-shaped flat wheat-egg noodles served in a clear, savoury ikan bilis (dried anchovy) broth. The noodles are rolled and either torn into irregular flat pieces (Mee Hoon Kueh style) or cut into wide flat ribbons, giving them a dense, chewy texture with a slightly lower glycaemic index than machine-extruded noodles. Standard toppings include seasoned minced pork, a whole soft-poached or raw-cracked egg, blanched sweet potato leaves (mani cai), and crispy fried ikan bilis. A dry version tossed in dark soy sauce and chilli is also widely available and adds approximately 200mg additional sodium. Per 500g bowl: approximately 420 kcal, 20g protein, 10g fat (3g saturated), 58g carbohydrate, 4g dietary fibre, and ~820mg sodium. GI is approximately 60 (medium); glycaemic load per bowl is ~34.8. Ban Mian is considered one of Singapore's most nutritionally balanced hawker noodle soups — the ikan bilis broth is lower in sodium than prawn-shell-based broths, the handmade noodles have a marginally lower GI than standard machine noodles, and the vegetables and egg contribute meaningful micronutrients including vitamin A, vitamin D, calcium, and iron. For hypertensive patients, Ban Mian is a preferred lower-sodium hawker noodle alternative. For Type 2 Diabetes patients, portion control of the noodle component and co-ingestion of protein and fat moderate the glycaemic response. The dish is dairy-free and widely available at Singapore hawker centres and coffee shops throughout the day.
common_user_queries: Ban Mian vs prawn noodle soup

confidence_reason: Core macros and secondary nutrients are sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo, the primary approved regional source for Singapore hawker dishes. The food is an exact match in the HPB database. Micronutrients for copper, manganese, and vitamin K are supplemented from USDA FoodData Central component-level data at confidence 0.9. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2008), clearly documented. Ontology codes are defensible closest-parent matches with gap notes. Multilingual naming covers all five mandatory languages for Singaporean dishes.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for Ban Mian as a composite dish is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central at the composite-dish level as of April 2026. water_g is set to -1: moisture content for this composite soup dish is not separately reported in the HPB source. 2. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per HPB Singapore source for this dish. 3. Copper (0.06mg/100g), manganese (0.08mg/100g), and vitamin K (12.0mcg/100g) are sourced from enriched JSON cross-reference with USDA FoodData Central ingredient-level data (wheat noodles, pork, egg, leafy greens, anchovies) at confidence 0.9; these values are not directly reported in the HPB composite entry. 4. GI ~60 is an analogous estimate — no direct GI measurement for Singapore-style Ban Mian exists in the University of Sydney GI Database; the estimate is based on wheat-egg flat noodle analogues (GI 57–65) from Atkinson et al. (2008). 5. Two portion sizes are noted in the input: 500g (HPB verified, default) and 450g (enriched_json); the 500g HPB-verified portion is used as the canonical serving size. 6. The dish is not certified halal as standard preparation includes pork; halal-certified Ban Mian variants exist at specific stalls but are not the generic standard.
